name: Pre-pregnancy cardiac output baseline
tier_rationale: Tier B from Mahendru et al. 2014 J Hypertension: prospective longitudinal cohort of 53 healthy nulliparous women from pre-conception through postpartum, inert-gas rebreathing for CO. Pre-conception baseline (~4.6 L/min) is the reference against which all pregnancy-induced changes are measured in this study. Single primary cohort; corroborated qualitatively by Sanghavi & Rutherford 2014 Circulation review.
notes: Cardiac output at the start of gestation, before pregnancy-induced rise.

name: Pre-pregnancy mean arterial pressure
tier_rationale: Tier B from Mahendru et al. 2014 J Hypertension: pre-conception MAP from brachial-cuff oscillometry in the longitudinal cohort. Consistent with population norms for nulliparous women aged 18-40.
notes: Mean arterial pressure at the start of gestation.

name: Pre-pregnancy uterine artery flow
tier_rationale: Tier B from Thaler et al. 1990 Am J Obstet Gynecol (PMID 2301480): transvaginal duplex-Doppler measurement, 94.5 mL/min in the left ascending uterine artery pre-pregnancy. Assuming bilateral symmetry gives ~189 mL/min total. NOTE the dataset's 50 mL/min is significantly lower than Thaler's pre-pregnancy bilateral estimate; this is flagged for human re-verification.

applicability.population: Healthy women, pre-pregnancy or early first trimester

notes: PRE-PREGNANCY UTERINE FLOW DISCREPANCY: dataset value (50 mL/min combined bilateral) is ~3.8x lower than what Thaler 1990's abstract reports (94.5 mL/min in left uterine artery alone, so ~189 mL/min bilateral). Either the dataset value reflects an alternate source (e.g. non-pregnant uterine-tissue blood flow as opposed to uterine-artery flow) or it is an extraction error. Needs human re-investigation against the published paper.

name: Between-individual sigma for cardiac output
tier_rationale: Tier C: between-individual sigma fraction. Hunter & Robson 1992 Heart (PMID 1467047) reviews the qualitative shape of maternal cardiac adaptation but does not directly report an individual-sigma figure. The 13% value is a model-fit abstraction from cohort variance reported across studies.
notes: Fractional one-sigma between-individual variation around the population cardiac-output trajectory (e.g. 0.13 = 13%).

name: Gestational week of peak cardiac output
tier_rationale: Tier B from Mahendru et al. 2014 longitudinal cohort. Peak CO around 28-32 weeks gestation; the precise peak depends on cohort definition and varies modestly across studies. The Mahendru 2014 cohort gives this as a central trend; replicated qualitatively in Sanghavi 2014 Circulation review.
notes: Gestational age (weeks) at which cardiac output reaches its maximum.

name: Cardiac-output trajectory spread parameter
tier_rationale: Tier C: Gaussian-shape spread parameter (weeks) fit to cohort CO trajectory data. Hunter & Robson 1992 documents the qualitative timeline (HR rises 2-5w, SV peaks mid-third tri); precise spread is derived from fitting the multi-study composite curve.
notes: Gaussian-curve spread (weeks) controlling how quickly cardiac output rises and falls around its peak. Caveats: Derived from a Gaussian-shape fit to the cohort curves; original sources report point estimates rather than a shape parameter.

name: Between-individual sigma for MAP
tier_rationale: Tier C: between-individual sigma for MAP. Sanghavi & Rutherford 2014 Circulation review documents the population-level MAP trajectory; individual-sigma is model-fit.
notes: One-sigma between-individual variation around the MAP trajectory.

name: Mid-pregnancy MAP nadir drop
tier_rationale: Tier B from Mahendru et al. 2014: mid-pregnancy MAP nadir represents the maximal systemic-vasodilation phase. Replicated qualitatively in Sanghavi 2014 review.
notes: Maximum drop in MAP from baseline during the mid-pregnancy systemic-vasodilation phase.

name: Gestational week of MAP nadir
tier_rationale: Tier B from Mahendru et al. 2014: gestational week at which MAP reaches its lowest point in the longitudinal cohort. The 20-24 week range is consistent across reviews.
notes: Gestational age (weeks) at which MAP reaches its lowest value.

name: MAP trajectory spread parameter
tier_rationale: Tier C: Gaussian shape parameter (weeks) for the MAP nadir trajectory. Derived from fitting the Sanghavi & Rutherford 2014 review's qualitative curves; not directly reported.
notes: Gaussian spread (weeks) controlling the width of the MAP nadir dip.

name: Peak gestational excess cardiac output
tier_rationale: Tier B from Mahendru et al. 2014: peak gestational excess CO above the pre-conception baseline, measured longitudinally in the same cohort via inert-gas rebreathing. Single primary cohort.
notes: MAHENDRU 2014 ABSTRACT TENSION: The Mahendru 2014 abstract (PMID 24406777) reports 'greatest increase in cardiac output occurred by the second trimester (Δ: 0.6 ± 1 l/min)'. The dataset's peak-excess of 2.7 L/min could be the cumulative preconception-to-peak rise rather than a per-visit increment, or could be from a different source. Needs human re-investigation against the paper's full results section.

name: Term uterine artery flow
tier_rationale: Tier B from Thaler et al. 1990 (PMID 2301480): transvaginal duplex-Doppler measurement, 342 mL/min in the left ascending uterine artery at late gestation. Bilateral estimate ~684 mL/min. The dataset's 750 mL/min central value is within plausible range of the doubled Thaler figure. Term uterine blood flow represents ~12% of maternal cardiac output.
notes: Combined bilateral uterine artery flow at term.

name: Uterine artery flow growth rate
tier_rationale: Tier C: logistic-growth rate parameter (1/week) for uterine blood-flow expansion. Fit from Thaler 1990 Doppler-cohort means at sampled gestational ages; not directly reported.
notes: Logistic growth-rate parameter for uterine artery flow expansion. Caveats: Fit parameter; not directly reported. Derived to match Doppler-cohort means at sampled gestational ages.

name: Between-individual sigma for uterine flow
tier_rationale: Tier C: between-individual sigma for uterine flow. Thaler 1990 reports population means and SDs; the fractional sigma is model-fit.
notes: Fractional one-sigma between-individual variation around the uterine-flow trajectory.

name: Pre-pregnancy resting heart rate
tier_rationale: Tier B from Mahendru et al. 2014 (PMID 24406777) prospective longitudinal cohort + Hunter & Robson 1992 (PMID 1467047) haemodynamic review. Pre-conception resting HR in healthy nulliparous women ~70 bpm, consistent with population norms for women aged 18-40.

extraction.method: triangulated; Mahendru 2014 abstract reports HR rises 13 ± 11 bpm to term, implying baseline near population norm

notes: Pre-pregnancy baseline. The first haemodynamic change in pregnancy is a rise in HR starting 2-5 weeks post-conception per Hunter & Robson 1992 abstract.

name: Term maternal heart rate
tier_rationale: Tier B from Mahendru et al. 2014 (PMID 24406777): the abstract reports 'heart rate increased maximally by the third trimester (Δ: 13 ± 11 bpm)'. Combined with the pre-conception baseline of ~70 bpm, term HR is ~85 bpm with combined uncertainty ~10 bpm. Replicated qualitatively in Hunter & Robson 1992 (PMID 1467047) and Sanghavi & Rutherford 2014.

extraction.method: triangulated from Mahendru 2014 abstract delta (+13 ± 11 bpm) applied to baseline

name: Pre-pregnancy resting stroke volume
tier_rationale: Tier C: pre-pregnancy SV ~65 mL derivable from baseline CO (~4.6 L/min) divided by baseline HR (~70 bpm). Hunter & Robson 1992 (PMID 1467047) abstract confirms SV 'increases slightly later than HR' through second trimester. Direct measurement in Mahendru 2014 cohort (n=54) but specific value not in abstract.

extraction.method: derived from baseline CO / HR; consistent with Hunter 1992 review

name: Heart rate rise from baseline to term
tier_rationale: Tier A from Mahendru 2014: HR rises ~15 bpm over gestation, peaks late T3; replicated across Sanghavi 2014 review.

name: Stroke volume rise from baseline to peak
tier_rationale: Tier B from Sanghavi 2014: SV rises ~15-20 mL during pregnancy; the increment is the dominant driver of the CO rise in early-mid pregnancy.

name: Maternal non-pregnant systemic vascular resistance
tier_rationale: Tier B from Sanghavi 2014 review: non-pregnant SVR ~1200-1400 dyn*s/cm^5 in healthy women; computed as MAP*80/CO.

name: Maternal term systemic vascular resistance
tier_rationale: Tier B from Sanghavi 2014: SVR falls ~25% during pregnancy (systemic vasodilation); ~950-1000 dyn*s/cm^5 at term.

name: Systemic vascular resistance, mid-pregnancy nadir
tier_rationale: Tier B from Sanghavi & Rutherford 2014: SVR falls from a non-pregnant baseline of ~1200 dyn·s·cm^-5 to a mid-pregnancy nadir of ~700-800 (a 30-40% drop), driven by relaxin/estrogen-mediated arterial vasodilation. The nadir typically occurs at ~24-28 weeks, paralleling the MAP nadir and the cardiac-output peak. Sits between baseline_svr_dyn_s_cm5 and term_svr_dyn_s_cm5 already in the dataset.
notes: Completes the SVR(t) trajectory anchor set together with the baseline + term values already in the dataset.

name: Pulmonary vascular resistance at term
tier_rationale: Tier B from Sanghavi & Rutherford 2014: PVR drops by ~30-40% through pregnancy from a non-pregnant baseline of ~120 dyn·s·cm^-5 to ~80 at term, paralleling the systemic vasodilation. The fall accommodates the rise in cardiac output without raising pulmonary artery pressure, an adaptation that fails in primary pulmonary hypertension — explaining why pregnancy is contraindicated in that condition.

name: Left ventricular mass, pre-pregnancy baseline
tier_rationale: Tier B from Sanghavi & Rutherford 2014: pre-pregnancy LV mass in healthy women of reproductive age is approximately 110-150 g by echocardiographic measurement. Acts as the anchor for the term LV-mass parameter to express the well-replicated ~30-50% pregnancy rise.

applicability.population: Healthy non-pregnant women of reproductive age

name: Left ventricular mass at term
tier_rationale: Tier B from Sanghavi & Rutherford 2014: LV mass rises by approximately 30-50% by term as eccentric hypertrophy adapts the ventricle to the chronic volume load of pregnancy. The remodelling regresses postpartum but slowly (months). Higher rises (>50%) are associated with peripartum cardiomyopathy in retrospective cohorts.
notes: Eccentric (volume-loading) rather than concentric (pressure-loading) hypertrophy — wall thickness rises modestly, chamber dimensions rise proportionally.

name: Aortic root diameter at term
tier_rationale: Tier C from Sanghavi & Rutherford 2014: aortic root diameter shows a small rise (~5-10%) by term, attributed to vessel-wall remodelling under sustained volume load and the hormonal effects of relaxin/estrogen on elastin. Tier C reflects the relatively small effect size against measurement noise.
notes: Background remodelling matters clinically because women with bicuspid aortic valve or Marfan syndrome may exceed surgical thresholds.

name: Aortic pulse wave velocity at term
tier_rationale: Tier C from Sanghavi & Rutherford 2014: aortic pulse wave velocity falls in mid-pregnancy (reflecting reduced arterial stiffness driven by relaxin/estrogen-mediated vessel-wall remodelling) and rises back toward or above non-pregnant baseline by term. Term values of ~7-8 m/s are typical; the trajectory is more reliable than any single timepoint.
notes: Mid-pregnancy nadir matters more than the term value clinically — sustained high PWV at mid-pregnancy is a hypertension-risk marker.
